text: The MSRE flush and fuel salt batches were fluorinated to recover 6.5 and $216\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium, respectively.
Known losses during processing were less than $0.1\%$ .
Gross beta and gamma decontamination factors of $1.2 \times 10^{9}$ and $8.6 \times 10^{8}$ were obtained.
Corrosion averaged about $0.1\mathrm{mil/hr}$ .
The Molten Salt Reactor Experiment (MSRE) is an 8-Mw circulating liquid fuel reactor operating at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The original fuel contained 65 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{LiF_4}$ , 30 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ , 5 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ and 0.9 mole $\%$ $^{238-235}\mathrm{UF_4}$ .
The reactor first went critical on June 1, 1965, and began full-power operation in May 1966.
Prior to shutdown on March 26, 1968, the reactor had operated for slightly more than one equivalent full-power year, or 72,400 Mwhr.

The MSRE Fuel Processing Facility was constructed in a small cell in the reactor building for two purposes: (1) to remove any accumulated oxides in the fuel or flush salt by $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ -HF sparging, and (2) to recover the original uranium charge from the salt to permit the addition of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fuel charge for the second phase of reactor operation.
The plant had been operated once before, in 1965, to remove oxide (115 ppm) from the flush salt before the reactor went critical.
A design and operations report describing the MSRE Fuel Processing Plant<sup>2</sup> was written before the initial test run; it was revised in

The uranium recovery process consists of fluorine sparging to volatilize the uranium, followed by decontamination of the gas stream with a $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ NaF bed and absorption of the $\mathrm{UF}_6$ on the $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ NaF beds.

After four months of testing and modifications, during which the fluorine disposal system was changed from the gas-phase reaction with $\mathrm{SO}_2$ to the aqueous KI-KOH scrubber, processing of the MSRE flush salt (66 mole % LiF - 34 mole % $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ ) began.
The flush salt was fluorinated to recover $6.5\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium.
About $216\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium was recovered from the MSRE fuel salt.
Corrosion of the fuel processing tank during fluorination of the fuel salt averaged about $0.1\mathrm{mil/hr}$ .
The most accurate calculations were based on the corrosion of nickel $(0.04\mathrm{mil/hr})$ and chromium $(0.14\mathrm{mil/hr})$ .
Fluorine utilization averaged $7.7\%$ during fluorination of the flush salt (and $39\%$ during fluorination of the flush salt) and $39\%$ during fluorination of the fuel salt.
The recovered uranium was decontaminated from fission products by gross gamma and gross beta decontamination factors of $8.6\times 10^{8}$ and $1.2\times 10^{9}$ , respectively.
Identifiable uranium losses were less than $0.1\%$ .

The transfer was made at 1000 to $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , a temperature that is well above the freezing point of the salt but not hot enough to reduce the strength of the metallic filter element below a safe limit.
The salt was then cooled to within $50^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ of the liquidus temperature to minimize corrosion and fission product volatilization during fluorination.
The salt was sparged with either pure fluorine or a fluorine-helium mixture at a relatively high flow rate ( $\sim 40$ liters/min) to convert the $\mathrm{UF_4}$ to $\mathrm{UF_5}$ .
When this occurred, the fluorine flow was reduced to 15 or 25 liters/min to increase the absorber residence time for more efficient absorption and to increase the fluorine utilization.
The gas passed through a $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ sodium fluoride trap where the chromium fluoride and most of the volatilized fission products, except iodine and tellurium, were retained.

A small fluorine flow was introduced upstream of this bed to ensure an excess of fluorine and to prevent any nonvolatile $\mathrm{UF}_6$ from forming and remaining on the heated NaF in the event that the fluorine utilization was near $100\%$ .
These absorbers were heated to 200 to $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to increase the reaction rate and to minimize $\mathrm{MoF_6}$ absorption.
The $\mathrm{UF_6}$ piping was heated to at least $140^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to prevent condensation of $\mathrm{UF_6}$ .

As the $\mathrm{UF_6}$ began to load on a given absorber and the temperature started to rise, the cooling air was turned on that particular absorber to limit the temperature to a maximum of $350^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The final absorber was operated below $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , where the partial pressure of $\mathrm{UF_6}$ over the $\mathrm{UF_6} \cdot 2$ NaF complex allowed only a negligible amount of uranium to reach the caustic scrubber.
If the gas stream leaving the absorbers contained greater than $50\%$ fluorine, it was diluted with helium before reaching the caustic scrubber.

The caustic scrubber was charged with 1300 liters of $2 \underline{\underline{M}}$ KOH -- $0.33 \underline{\underline{M}}$ KI containing $0.2 \underline{\underline{M}}$ $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{B}_4\mathrm{O}_7$ , which was added as a soluble neutron poison.

scrubber; the oxygen amounted to about $25\%$ of the fluorine flow to the scrubber.

It was monitored for gamma activity and iodine before being mixed with the rest of the building exhaust gas, which passed through additional filters and was finally discharged from a 100-ft-tall stack.
The uranium concentration in the salt was expected to be less than 50 ppm at this time.
Since nickel is more noble than iron or chromium, it was reduced by hydrogen sparging at $1225^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
In this operation, the $\mathrm{ZrF}_4$ concentration of the salt was increased by a negligible amount ( $< 1\%$ ).
storage tank (fluorinator), the $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ NaF trap, the caustic scrubber, two remotely-operated air valves, three salt freeze valves, and the blower for the absorber cubicle.
The shielding around the fuel processing cell was designed to limit the radiation level to 5 mrad/hr when a fully irradiated, 3-day-decayed fuel batch was in the fuel storage tank.
To meet this requirement, new roof plugs were installed, and the east and west walls of the cell were backed up by an additional 2 ft of stacked barytes block.
Additional shielding was designed for 30-day-decayed fuel, but was not installed because the plant testing required more time than expected.
(3) an NaF trap in the fuel processing cell - to shield the electrical insulation and valve diaphragms for absorbed $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ .

The fuel storage tank is heated by four sets of heaters, which provide $5.8\mathrm{kw}$ on the bottom, $11.6\mathrm{kw}$ on the lower sides, $5.8\mathrm{kw}$ on the upper sides, and $2\mathrm{kw}$ on the top.

During the period in which $\mathrm{UF}_4$ was converted to $\mathrm{UF}_5$ (when the fluorine utilization was high), the heat of reaction, plus the afterheat, provided sufficient heat to maintain the salt at $850^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
About $12\mathrm{kw}$ of electrical heat was required during the reduction operation at $1225^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The gas inlet line for the fuel storage tank has a normal submergence of 64 in., and the differential pressure between this line and the gas space provides an indication of liquid level.
4.14) to check the weigh cell calibration during the filling and emptying operations.
An automatic valve will open in the tank off-gas line if the tank pressure reaches 50 psig.
An alarm sounds at 5 psig, which is a pressure higher than that normally found during operation.
6 is operated at 750 to $800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The slightly oversized line from the fuel storage tank is also heated to $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to prevent the absorption of uranium at the trap inlet.
The trap has two sets of heaters that are separately controlled, 2.25 kw in the center pipe and 9 kw on the sides.
There are three thermocouples located in wells at the inlet, center, and outlet of the trap, respectively, which are maintained at temperatures within $10^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ of each other.
With 4 in. of insulation, the heat loss is about 1270 w at $775^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Chromium fluoride volatilization was expected as the uranium concentration in the salt decreased; but such volatilization was apparently almost insignificant until the concentration became as low as 20 ppm.
7 was used for disposal of excess fluorine and of the HF produced during $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ reduction.
The Monel spray ring distributor originally installed in the tank was replaced with two dip lines, each with three 3/8-in. holes, as shown in the figure.
On one occasion, in which the temperature was allowed to increase from less than $80^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to greater than $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , no decrease in plugging was noted.

The mist filter is a high-efficiency Fiberglas air filter consisting of fifty-five 9-1/2-in.-diam by 28-in.-long bags hanging vertically in a 30-in.-diam by 4-ft-high stainless steel vessel.
The total filter area is about $50\text{ft}^2$ .
8, was charged with activated alumina when it was used as the fluorine disposal backup trap for the $\mathrm{SO}_2$ system.

To prevent excessive pressure drop with a gas having a high fluorine content, the soda-lime was mixed with an equal volume of 4- to 8-mesh alumina, which has a very slow reaction rate at low temperatures.
9 were considerably overdesigned to permit the handling of the large amount of iodine that is present in 30-day-decayed fuel, and to allow for the uncertainties associated with a gas
The original trap (inlet) was initially charged with replaceable cannisters, each of which had a total bed depth of 1-1/2 in.

9, were used during the fluorine reactor tests described in Sect.
The fuel processing cell ventilation air and the gas stream from the charcoal traps pass through a 2-in.-deep, 24- by 24-in.
Fiberglas prefilter and a 11-1/2-in.-deep, 24- by 24-in.
There are three 2-in.-diam butterfly valves for isolation and for bypassing the filters during replacement of the latter.
During the processing described in this report, the differential pressure varied between 1/2 and 1 in. $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ .
Space and piping are available to connect two 15,000-liter fluorine tanks mounted on trailers simultaneously.
The tanks can be initially loaded with fluorine under a pressure of 70 psig.
This pressure is reduced to 18 psig by a throttling valve that is controlled automatically from the operating panelboard.
This pressure regulator is set to provide a constant 15-psig pressure upstream of the flow control valve (which is controlled from the panelboard).
Helium is obtained from the reactor system helium supply, which consists of a 39,000-ft³ tank mounted on a trailer.
The 2400 psig pressure is reduced to 20 psig at the fuel processing system panelboard.
10), connected in series, are located in a 9 ft 4 in. x 2 ft 4 in. x 2 ft 2 in. sealed cubicle located in the operating area above the processing cell.

The cubicle is vented to the cell, where a blower is located for providing additional negative pressure (below the -0.3 in. $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ in the cell) when air is being used to cool the absorbers.
This blower failed during fuel salt run 1 (Sect.
When loaded to within 1/2 in. of the top, each absorber holds about $25\mathrm{kg}$ of NaF.
The minimum cross-sectional area (outlet side) is $0.46\mathrm{ft}^2$ .
4.14) are also located in the cubicle.
Each heater was used to heat each absorber to about $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ before the start of $\mathrm{UF}_6$ absorption.
The sampler, which is mounted on a semipermanent roof plug near the instrument panelboard, is connected to the tank by a vertical 1-1/2-in. pipe.
During processing, the sampler line was purged by a minimum of 5 std liters of helium per minute, mainly to prevent the condensation of $\mathrm{UF}_6$ on the cold surfaces.
temperatures of $550^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and $130^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , respectively, at positions 3 ft above the tank and 6 ft above the tank (near the bottom of the roof plugs).

Under the conditions prevalent during the processing of the fuel salt, condensation of $\mathrm{UF}_6$ would not occur above a temperature of $115^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , which would probably be near the temperature of the isolation valve in the operating area.
It was set to alarm when the radiation level reached 100 mr/hr.
The replaceable filter element, which rests on a spherical seal, is mounted in a heated, insulated 6-in.-diam, 7-ft-long pipe.
The 4-ft-long filter consists of two concentric fibrous metal cylinders with a total filter area of 8.65 ft².
The upper 3 ft of the filter housing is normally unheated and provides a transition zone from the molten salt to the cool ring joint seal flange, which contains a continuously pressured leak monitor.
The center of the filter element is a sealed can to limit the volume of salt runback that is unavailable for processing (0.56 ft³) on transfer
The element will lift off the seat when a salt pressure of about 1 psi (depending on salt density) is applied under the seat.
The outer element has a burst strength of 25 psi at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
This plate provides a collapse strength of 131 psi, which is well above the maximum transfer pressure of about 30 psi.
During filtration of the flush and fuel salts, no pressure drop across the filter was detected, even though salts containing about $10\mathrm{kg}$ of reduced metals were filtered.
Filtration time was about 2 hr for each batch.
Later, $2\mathrm{ft}^3$ of fuel salt was returned to the processing tank to be used for a salt distillation experiment.
Two detectors (0 to 2 and 0 to 10 equivalent cfm of air) were required upstream to obtain both range and sensitivity, while one 0- to 2-cfm detector was required downstream.
To prevent plugging of the 0.03-in. capillaries by NaF particles, the primary elements are protected by $36-\mu$ Feltmetal filters.

The two inlet meters are heated by a heating tape (that is controlled separately from the rest of the gas line heaters) to prevent the temperature on the electrical insulation from exceeding $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
These flow meters agreed within $\pm 4\%$ of the helium rotameters when only helium was present.

At the end of run 6, the exit meter indicated a $\mathrm{MoF}_6$ (which also has a high heat capacity) rate corresponding to a molybdenum corrosion rate of almost 1 mil/hr, while the inlet meter throughout the run indicated a corrosion rate only slightly greater than the 0.2 mil/hr rate assumed for molybdenum.
The $\mathrm{UF}_6$ flow at a given time could be calculated from the following formula, assuming a $\mathrm{MoF}_6$ flow rate equivalent to a molybdenum corrosion rate of 0.2 mil/hr:

In April 1968 (about two weeks after the reactor shutdown), the fluorine supply and excess fluorine disposal systems, which had not previously been operated, were tested.

13) at temperatures above $400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to form sulfuryl fluoride, $\mathrm{SO}_2\mathrm{F}_2$ , a relatively inert gas that could be safely passed through Fiberglas filters and discharged to the atmosphere.
Results of four fluorine disposal test runs are summarized in Table 4 and Fig.
The first run was started on April 2, but was interrupted after about $1 - 1 / 2$ hr by fluorine ignition of two Teflon plug valves.

A total of 900 liters of fluorine had been used; however, the flow rate was very erratic because of the difficulty encountered in blending a low fluorine flow rate ( $\sim 7.5$ std liters/min) with a high helium flow rate ( $\sim 85$ std liters/min) to obtain dilute fluorine for conditioning the system.
During the time that the fluorine was flowing, there was no evidence of a reaction in the fluorine reactor although the alumina bed was heated higher than $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
After the flow of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and fluorine was stopped, the temperature of the fluorine reactor rose to $520^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
When the $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and fluorine flows were started, the reactor temperature reading went off scale ( $>1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) because of insufficient cooling from low-pressure steam.
(1) Finer-mesh alumina and a heated alumina bed to increase the capacity and the reaction rate; additional thermocouples to permit the consumption of fluorine in the bed to be followed.

Test run 2 was then started on May 1, 1968.

About $1/2$ hr after the start of fluorine flow, the reactor inlet temperature started to rise, indicating a reaction; however, temperature points 16 in. and 32 in. downstream but still in the reaction zone) showed practically no heating.

The alumina trap was being heated at the rate of about $80^{\circ}\mathrm{F/hr}$ , but the temperature at a point 8 in. down the bed rose sharply, from $400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to $>1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in less than 15 min, about 2 hr after the start of fluorine flow.
Two hours later, the temperature measured at points farther down the alumina bed rose from about $800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to $>1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

(Later, such a reaction was shown to occur above $800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .) However, because at this time we were unaware of the $\mathrm{SO}_2\mathrm{F}_2$ -alumina reaction, efforts were directed to improving $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and $\mathrm{F}_2$ flow control.
About 100 min after the start of fluorine flow, $30\mathrm{mc}$ of $\mathrm{CH}_3^{13}\mathrm{I}$ was injected into the gas stream upstream of the charcoal traps.

Although fluorine flow was stopped $1\mathrm{hr}$ after the start of iodide injection, the fluorine reactor temperature remained above $400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for 4 additional hours (from purged fluorine).

After the first 72 min of the 2-hr iodide injection period, the summation of the levels indicated by the five radiation monitors on the charcoal traps reached a maximum and leveled off, indicating all the iodide had been loaded.
9) and a corresponding reduction at CT-C.

During this time, the temperature at the outlet of the alumina exceeded $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and there was some heating of the charcoal, indicating a reaction with fluorine (probably from $\mathrm{SO}_2\mathrm{F}_2$ decomposition).
The almost immediate penetration of some of the iodide through the first 12 in. of charcoal was not unexpected, as this type of behavior has been noted previously for organic iodides.

After the third run, the insulation was removed from the fluorine reactor to allow the cooling coils to be examined. [The copper coils were bonded to the Monel reactor with E-Z flow solder (flow point, $1328^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) containing cadmium.

At temperatures above $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , alloying, along with the formation of cracks and weakening of the metal, can occur.] In runs 1 and 3, the thermowell temperatures exceeded $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (maximum recorder temperature) for 3-1/2 hr and for 40 min, respectively.
Examination revealed that the solder had melted, leaving the middle third of the 44-in. coil unbonded.
Run 4A was started on May 20, 1968.

A preheater had been installed on the fluorine reactor inlet to provide inlet temperatures above $400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to ensure a reaction at low fluorine flows as well as at the start of fluorine flow.
A preheater had also been installed at the alumina trap inlet to obtain more satisfactory temperature distribution and to improve the efficiency at the bed inlet (in run 3 no reaction occurred in the
The ranges on the two temperature recorders were increased from 0 - 1000 to 0 - 1500 and 0 - $2000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A temporary bypass line was installed around the fuel storage tank to eliminate its $120\text{-ft}^3$ volume between the fluorine supply and the fluorine reactor.
The heated zone proceeded to the second thermowell, and the fluorine flow was stopped when an off-scale reading of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was obtained at this point.
The system was conditioned so that automatic flow control of $100\%$ fluorine could then be used.
The fourth test was resumed on May 21; our efforts were centered on trying to determine the cause of the alumina trap heating.

To ensure a complete reaction of the fluorine with $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , the fluorine reactor inlet was preheated so that the temperature of the reaction zone was about $600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ; steam cooling was delayed until the reactor temperature reached $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Then the steam pressure was carefully adjusted to maintain all temperatures above $400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A maximum fluorine flow of 25 std liter/min was used, with a minimum of $50\%$ excess $\mathrm{SO}_2$ .
When the exit of the alumina trap reached about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , iodide began to move down the charcoal bed.
Most of the iodine left the first 12 in. of bed and accumulated at the inlet of the backup trap.
Fluorine flow was stopped 20 min after this migration began, and thus no iodine left the trap.
About 5 mc of iodide still remained at this time.
Laboratory tests $^{4}$ were made by W.
The movement was probably not due to $\mathrm{SO}_2\mathrm{F}_2$ since Pappas' work showed only partial trapping of $\mathrm{SO}_2\mathrm{F}_2$ below $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Also, $\mathrm{SO}_2\mathrm{F}_2$ was produced for 2 hr before the alumina bed temperature reached $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and no iodine movement was observed during this time.
With steam cooling of the fluorine reactor, it was not possible to maintain the reactor walls above $400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
If work had been continued on the $\mathrm{SO}_2$ system, we would have installed controlled electric heat over the entire 8 ft of the reactor instead of steam heat over the first 3-2/3 ft.
Future work would also have involved decreasing activated alumina trap temperatures (to 700 to $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) to prevent the $\mathrm{SO}_2\mathrm{F}_2$ reaction.
The scrubber was charged with a 2 M KOH -- 0.33 M KI solution, and 30 mc of $\mathrm{CH}_3^{13}\mathrm{I}$ was again loaded on the charcoal traps.
The backup alumina trap material was replaced, and the trap was heated to $700 - 800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Fluorine diluted to less than $50\%$ with helium was used to condition the scrubber -- an operation requiring several hours.

After about 50 min of conditioning the new or recently exposed piping with dilute fluorine the fluorine flow rate was stabilized at 35 std liters/min and diluted with an equal flow of helium upstream of the scrubber.
As soon as the fluorine flow began, the temperature near the exit of the alumina trap (containing 5-mesh alumina) began to increase slowly.
This was verified two days later by injection of 6 std liters of oxygen per minute into the system, using a helium flow equal to that used during the test run.
A contact microphone was installed on the scrubber tank to detect any abnormal reaction such as that which occurred in laboratory tests when a fluorine concentration of greater than $50\%$ was used.
A second scrubber test was made on July 4, 1968.
The fuel storage tank bypass line was removed, and the tank was heated to $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for a pressure test.
After 70 min of fluorine flow, the pressure upstream of the scrubber started to increase.
The flow was, at first, reduced from 35 to 25 std liters/min and, then, had to be stopped completely 15 min later to prevent the solution from leaving the scrubber tank.
A $50\text{-ft}^2$ filter was, therefore, installed downstream of the scrubber to remove such material.
To prevent plugging of the connecting line, the size of the line was increased from 3/4-in. to 1-1/2 in., and all sharp bends were eliminated.

Heating and insulating the absorbers provided two advantages: (1) the amount of $\mathrm{UF_6}$ leaving a particular absorber before that absorber is fully loaded was reduced, and (2) the amount of coabsorbed molybdenum was minimized.
On August 1, the flush salt was transferred by gas pressure, from the fuel flush tank in the drain cell to the fuel storage tank in the fuel processing cell.
The transfer required about $11 - 1 / 2$ hr.
Each of these flushing operations contributed about $200~\mathrm{ppm}$ (or $1\mathrm{kg}$ ) of uranium to the flush salt, for a total of about $6 - 1 / 2\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium.
Pertinent irradiation and decay data are shown in Table 5.
flowmeter rose rapidly and reached a maximum after 48 min of fluorine sparging.
The peak reading corresponded to a fluorine utilization of about $15\%$ .
2 absorbers rose, but lagged the initial rise in the mass flowmeter reading by 20 to 30 min.
It was finally concluded that $\mathrm{MoF_6}$ was the cause of the high reading; therefore, fluorine sparging was resumed after 95 min.
After a total of 5 hr of fluorine sparging, the fluorine supply was exhausted.

Fluorination was resumed after the caustic solution was replenished; it was then terminated when a uranium analysis of 24 ppm was obtained for the flush salt, since fluorine sparging had been carried out for an additional 109 min and the reading of the inlet mass flowmeter had become constant.
Analysis of a second salt sample, taken at this time indicated a uranium concentration of only 7 ppm (desired <10 ppm).
The absorbers were loaded with low-surface-area NaF for the flush salt processing and were heated to 200 - $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to increase the reaction rate (see Sect.
However, almost $30\%$ was collected on the second absorber and possibly $3\%$ on the third, although no temperature rise was detected on the last three absorbers.
The temperature rise on the first absorber was only $39^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ; thus cooling air was not required.
After the analysis of the first fluorination salt sample (uranium, 24 ppm) was obtained, heatup of the salt for hydrogen reduction was started.
A period of 43 hr at maximum wattage was required to raise the salt temperature from 860 to $1220^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
August 5, 1968, hydrogen sparging was started.
A mixture of hydrogen (30 std liters/min) and helium (20 std liters/min) was used in conjunction with a 10-standard-liter/min helium sample line purge.
After 2 hr, the rate of increase of the monitor was essentially zero, which indicated a nickel reduction rate of less than 3 ppm/hr.
Hydrogen sparging was continued for a total of 10.8 hr.
The salt was then sparged with a mixture of hydrogen (30 std liters/min) and helium (10 std liters/min).
An additional flow of helium (20 std liters/min) was used to purge the sample line.
After 9 hr of sparging, the hydrogen flow was stopped; then the system was purged with (75 std liters/min) for 85 min before the salt was sampled.
Analyses of the samples indicated that, although the nickel concentration had been reduced to less than 50 ppm, there had only been a slight reduction in the iron and chromium concentrations.
An additional $470\mathrm{g}$ of zirconium was added, and the salt was sparged with the same hydrogen-helium mixture (see above) for an additional 16 hr.

(1) Enough high-surface-area NaF (1400 lb) was procured from ORGDP to load the last three absorbers in each run, and the first two absorbers in three of the six runs, to obtain a comparison between the performance of the two types of NaF.
(4) For more efficient absorber purging, a connection was provided at the absorber inlet to permit purging with pure helium instead of purging through the fuel storage tank.
(5) A small soda-lime trap was installed in the absorber cubicle to allow the absorbers to be vented to atmospheric pressure instead of the scrubber liquid head.
The transfer of the fuel salt to the processing cell was started on August 17, 1968, after a difficulty with a restriction in the line at the cell wall penetration was resolved.
The spare heaters at the penetration were energized, thereby raising the temperature from $1108^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $1255^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , but the restriction persisted.
This operation required about 11 hr at an average rate of 15 lb/min.
After the transfer, a radiation level of about 2 r/hr was detected at the filter located upstream of the inlet mass flowmeter in the absorber cubicle.
This radioactivity was caused by carryover of metallic ${}^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ during the gas blowthrough at the end of the salt transfer.
The filter was decontaminated to approximately 100 mr/hr to minimize contamination of the uranium product during fluorination.
Analysis of the decontamination solutions confirmed that the principal activity present was ${}^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ .
Because the fluorine supply system had been opened, it seemed advisable to condition this equipment with dilute fluorine (this was started on August 23).
After 30 min at a flow rate of 20 std liters/min, the fluorine flow was increased to 40 std liters/min for the conversion of $\mathrm{UF_4}$ to $\mathrm{UF_5}$ .
Fluorine utilization was very high ( $\sim 70\%$ ) during this period.
For almost 6 hr, the absorber inlet and outlet meters read the same, indicating that no absorbable compounds were volatilized.
For the next 100 min, the readings on both meters rose (the inlet meter at the faster rate), but no absorber heating was noted.
After 7-1/2 hr, $\mathrm{UF_6}$ absorption was indicated by a temperature rise on the first absorber.
Twenty minutes before $\mathrm{UF_6}$ absorption began, the pressure in the absorber cubicle rose above the alarm point (-1 in. $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ ).
After run 6, analysis of a fuel salt sample indicated 26 ppm, or about $130\mathrm{g}$ , of uranium remaining in the salt.
The salt was, therefore, heated to $1225^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to reduce the structural metal fluorides.
The temperature of the fuel salt reached $1225^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ on August 31, 1968 and a 30-standard-liter/min hydrogen sparge was started at this time.
A low-flow hydrogen sparge had been used during the previous 15-hr heatup.
The unshielded radiation level at the cubicle wall was about 80 r/hr.
The source of this radiation was later identified as ${}^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ (Sect.
After reactor shutdown, the $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ grew back in from $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}$ decay.
After a decay period of five months, less than $0.1\%$ of this $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ would be required to produce the observed radiation level.
About $60\%$ of the $\mathrm{NbF}_5$ formed during fluorination would be carried from the salt by 60,000 liters of gas flow.

Because of the higher concentration of corrosion products in the fuel salt (than in the flush salt), a 24-hr hydrogen sparge was specified; however, frequent scrubber dip line plugging caused the sparge to be stopped after 17 hr.
Because of frequent plugging of the lines, the flow rates were reduced below the planned 30 std liters/min for about $70\%$ of the time.
As expected, there was no evidence of reduction of chromium or iron; however, the analysis showed that the nickel concentration had increased from 840 ppm to 1540 ppm.
After another 17 hr of sparging, the nickel concentration had only decreased to 520 ppm; again no chromium or iron was reduced.
A third period of sparging for 17-1/2 hr decreased the nickel concentration further to 180 ppm.

Twenty-seven zirconium slugs, weighing an average of $180\mathrm{g}$ each, were charged through the sampler; two others rolled beyond the reach of the manipulator and could not be charged to the salt at this time.
Approximately $5\mathrm{kg}$ of zirconium was added, and the salt was sparged for 24 hr with hydrogen at the rate of 10 std liters/min.
Analysis of a filtered sample of salt taken at this time showed the nickel to be completely reduced and the chromium and iron reduced to about $100~\mathrm{ppm}$ .
The salt had been agitated with helium prior to the sample analysis and then was sparged for 8 additional hr with hydrogen.
A dip sample from the drain tank showed the chromium concentration to be 34 ppm; a second filtered sample showed only 29 ppm of chromium in solution.
The fluorine consumed by corrosion is assumed to be constant at 2.5 std liters/min.
Laboratory tests had shown a decrease in utilization from 8.0 to $3.8\%$ as the temperature was reduced from $930^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to $840^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The higher utilization (average, $39\%$ ) during the fuel salt processing was probably due both to lower gas velocities by a factor of 5 and greater dip tube submergence (by a factor of 15) than used in laboratory tests.

Utilization was very high before the start of $\mathrm{UF}_6$ volatilization (average, $71\%$ ) and, after that, seemed to be relatively insensitive to uranium concentration until nearly all the uranium was volatilized (see Figs.
The highest average utilization during $\mathrm{UF}_6$ volatilization occurred in the fifth run (see Table 6).
The fluorine flow rates calculated from the decrease in pressure in the fluorine trailers agree reasonably well with the results obtained with the orifice flowmeter except in runs 2 and 5.

In run 2 a fluorine flow of $90\%$ of the rate indicated by the flowmeter was used since this produces: (1) utilizations in reasonable agreement with those observed in the other runs, (2) an integrated $\mathrm{UF}_6$ flow from the inlet meter that is in agreement with the absorber weight increase, and (3) a reasonable amount of $\mathrm{MoF}_6$ leaving the absorbers as calculated from the outlet meter.
In run 5 a fluorine flow of $110\%$ of the rate indicated by the flowmeter was used.
The corrosion rates shown in Table 7 were calculated from the increase in Cr, Fe, and Ni contents of the salt and from the amount of $\mathrm{MoF_6}$ volatilized.

The corrosion rates calculated during flush salt processing may be high since they include a 2-hr conditioning period at $1125^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with fluorine.
All other fluorine exposures were at 850 to $860^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The corrosion due to molybdenum in the flush salt could not be calculated directly because of the short time interval (20 min) between the two indications.
The equivalent time interval for the fuel salt was 90 min; thus the calculation in this case should be reasonably accurate.
A 15-min time lag in the temperature response was assumed in the calculations.
The average exit meter reading agreed reasonably well with the calculated reading for $43\%$ fluorine utilization (see Table 6).
(Chromium fluoride is not volatilized until essentially all of the $\mathsf{UF}_6$ has been volatilized.) As seen in Table 8, $\mathsf{MoF}_6$ is somewhat more volatile than $\mathsf{UF}_6$ .
The molybdenum complex, $\mathsf{MoF}_6\cdot 3\mathsf{NaF}$ , is much less stable than the uranium complex, $\mathsf{UF}_6\cdot 3\mathsf{NaF}$ .

Run 6 appears to be an exception in this respect.
Since the MSRE fuel salt contained 66 wt $\%$ $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ , a significant amount of $^{239}\mathrm{Pu}$ was produced.
At the time of reactor shutdown, the plutonium concentration in the fuel salt was calculated to be $112\mathrm{ppm}$ ( $560\mathrm{g}$ ).
Five salt samples withdrawn after fluorination, after reduction, and after return of the salt batch to the reactor drain tank showed an average concentration of $110\mathrm{ppm}$ .

Figure 21 shows the buildup of $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ after reactor shutdown and the buildup after fluorination, assuming complete volatilization.
At least $60\%$ of the grown-in niobium must have been lost during sample preparation (before analysis).

Figure 20 indicates that approximately $60\%$ of the niobium in the fuel salt volatilized during fluorination.
From the lack of heat generation in the NaF trap, the volatilized niobium is believed to constitute less than $10\%$ of the $9 \times 10^{4}$ curies which would have grown in after reactor shutdown.

(A temperature rise of $20^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ would have been observed from the heat generation from 9000 curies of $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ .) The temperature of the trap, remained constant; that is, no heater adjustment was required through-out the six runs.
6.8) rather than niobium that was volatilized from the salt passing through the NaF trap.
Two days after the end of fluorination, at the beginning of hydrogen reduction, a large amount of niobium (estimated to be 15 to 20 curies) was found on the absorber filter.
Since $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ should have grown in at the rate of about 1000 curies per day, apparently less than $1\%$ of the niobium in the salt was carried to the absorber cubicle.
A gamma scan, made of the filter on March 10, 1969, is shown in Fig.
The only definite peak is that of $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ at 0.77 Mev.
Very small amounts of $^{125}\mathrm{Sb}$ , $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ , and $^{106}\mathrm{Rh}$ could be the cause of the bulge in the curve between 0.4 and 0.55 Mev.
A total of 404 mc of $^{131}\mathrm{I}$ was calculated to be in the fuel salt at the start of processing.
Analyses of the caustic scrubber solutions showed that 288 mc was collected during run 1 and 10 mc was collected during run 2 for a $74\%$ accountability.
This is in reasonable agreement with the results of iodine analyses of fuel salt (during reactor operation), which showed about $65\%$ of the calculated amount.
The discrepancy between the calculated amount and the analyzed amount is caused by the loss of metallic $^{131}\mathrm{Te}$ before decay.
Freeze-valve samples showed less than $1\%$ remaining in the salt.
Since $^{129}\mathrm{Te}$ has no long-lived precursor, the activity will not grow back in after reactor shutdown.

However, since analyses of the scrubber solutions showed tellurium to be below the limit of detection, it is probable that less than $1\%$ was present in the salt at reactor shutdown.
Any tellurium passing through the scrubber would have been removed by the activated alumina<sup>9</sup> in the soda-lime trap.

5.5, two types of NaF were used: a high-surface-area (HSA) material $(1\mathrm{m}^2 /\mathrm{g})$ , prepared at ORGDP by heating $\mathrm{NaHF}_2$ pellets, and a low-surface-area (LSA) material $(0.063\mathrm{m}^2 /\mathrm{g}$ , prepared at the UCNC Paducah Plant, from NaF powder and water and sintered at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The LSA material was originally specified for the MSRE Fuel Processing Plant because of its higher capacity for both $\mathrm{CrF}_5$ (on the $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ NaF trap) and for $\mathrm{UF}_6$ .
5.2) suggested that the reaction rate with $\mathrm{UF}_6$ might be too slow for complete uranium absorption under the planned operating conditions.
Subsequent laboratory tests<sup>10</sup> confirmed the much slower reaction rate with the LSA material.

In spite of the fact that the LSA NaF absorbers were operated at higher temperatures (to compensate for the slower reaction rate), the total loading was 13 to $14\%$ higher.
The maximum uranium loading obtained was $0.56\mathrm{g}$ per g of NaF, as compared with $0.49\mathrm{g}$ per g of NaF for the HSA material.
23 for the LSA NaF contained from 11 to $18\%$ HSA (added later to more fully charge the absorbers), which undoubtedly reduced the loading on these absorbers.

The first absorber in run 1 had an unusually short breakthrough time (25 sec), which was possibly caused by absorption of $\mathrm{MoF}_6$ that had been volatilized before the start of $\mathrm{UF}_6$ volatilization (Sect.
The inlet of the first absorber in each run was analyzed for gross beta and gamma activity about 12 days after fluorination; the results are shown in Fig.
At the time of the analyses, the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ daughters had reached equilibrium, while the $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ daughters had attained only about $30\%$ of equilibrium.

(The activity in the feed salt before fluorination, counted at the same time, was $2.66 \times 10^{13}$ gross gamma and $3.8 \times 10^{13}$ gross beta counts per minute per gram of uranium.) The average gross beta and gamma decontamination factors (DF) for the six absorbers analyzed, after correction for uranium daughter activity, were $1.2 \times 10^{9}$ and $8.6 \times 10^{8}$ respectively.
The only activity collected in any measurable amount on the NaF absorbers with the UF $_6$ was $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ .

Because of this contamination, the decontamination factor for $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ $\left(\frac{\mathrm{dpm per gram of U in salt}}{\mathrm{dpm per gram of U on absorber}}\right)$ was lower than the actual DF from niobium volatilized during fluorination and collected on the absorbers.
The DF was lower at the start of run 1 when most of the niobium between the NaF trap and the absorbers was fluorinated and collected on the first absorber.
The DF's varied from $5 \times 10^6$ at the start to $1 \times 10^{10}$ at the end of fluorination.
Therefore, notwithstanding the piping contamination, a considerably higher niobium DF was obtained in these runs than in the Volatility Pilot Plant ( $5 \times 10^7$ ) for 30-day-decayed uranium.
Laboratory-scale runs have shown decontamination factors of 10 to $10^{3}$ for this operation.[11]
